subject: Physics
q: The temperature at which the kinetic energy of oxygen molecules becomes double than its value at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is
question: The temperature at which the kinetic energy of oxygen molecules becomes double than its value at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is
solution:
The kinetic energy is given by KE=f2kT.

Let the kinetic energy be K1 at 27 °C and the kinetic energy be K' at T' temperature. As given in the question, K'=2K1. Hence, 

K'K1=T'T⇒2=T'300⇒T'=600 K

In Celsius the temperature is T'-273=327 °C.

Tmean α1T
∴ time =V4π2r2VN
V=2RTπM

Its <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> value will be:

=n52R+2n72Rn32R+2n52R

subject: Physics
q: A vessel contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of nitrogen gas at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The amount of heat to be transferred to the gas to double the r.m.s. speed of its molecules will be : (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>32</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A vessel contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of nitrogen gas at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The amount of heat to be transferred to the gas to double the r.m.s. speed of its molecules will be : (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>32</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The RMS speed of a gas at a given temperature is given by, vrms=3RTM.

Therefore, to double the RMS speed, the temperature should be increased to four times the initial temperature. Therefore,

Tf=1200 K, Ti=300 K, and n=1428=12

The volume of the vessel will remain the same therefore, the process is an isochoric process. Therefore,

Q=nCvΔT=12×5R2×900

⇒Q=9360 J

subject: Physics
q: The r.m.s. speed of oxygen molecules at $47^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is equal to that of the hydrogen molecules kept at $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Mass of oxygen molecule/mass of hydrogen molecule $=32 / 2$)
question: The r.m.s. speed of oxygen molecules at $47^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is equal to that of the hydrogen molecules kept at $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Mass of oxygen molecule/mass of hydrogen molecule $=32 / 2$)
solution:
The root-mean-square speed of gas molecules is: $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}}$, where $T$ is absolute temperature and $M$ is molar mass.

For oxygen at $T_1 = 47°C = 320$ K with $M_{O_2} = 32$ g/mol:
$v_{rms,O_2} = \sqrt{\frac{3R(320)}{32}}$

For hydrogen with $M_{H_2} = 2$ g/mol at temperature $T_2$:
$v_{rms,H_2} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT_2}{2}}$

Equating the RMS speeds:
$\sqrt{\frac{3R(320)}{32}} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT_2}{2}}$

$\frac{320}{32} = \frac{T_2}{2}$

$T_2 = 2 \times 10 = 20$ K

Converting to Celsius: $t_2 = 20 - 273 = -253°C$, which is option (2).

subject: Physics
q: A flask contains Hydrogen and Argon in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass. The temperature of the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of the two gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>argon</mi></msub><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>hydrogen</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is: (Given : Atomic Weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>39</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math>)
question: A flask contains Hydrogen and Argon in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass. The temperature of the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of the two gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>argon</mi></msub><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>hydrogen</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is: (Given : Atomic Weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>39</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn></math>)
solution:
The formula to calculate the average kinetic energy per molecule of a gas at any temperature T is given by

Kavg=32kBT

where, kB is Boltzmann constant.

From above equation, it can be concluded that the average kinetic energy per molecule is independent of the molecular weight of the gas.

Hence, the required ratio is 1:1.

subject: Physics
q: During an adiabatic process, if the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature, then the ratio of $\frac{C_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}$ for the gas is :
question: During an adiabatic process, if the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature, then the ratio of $\frac{C_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}$ for the gas is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T}^3 \\
& \mathrm{PT}^{-3}=\text { constant } \\
& \because \frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{nR}=\text { constant from ideal gas equation } \\
& (\mathrm{P})(\mathrm{PV})^{-3}=\text { constant } \\
& \mathrm{P}^{-2} \mathrm{~V}^{-3}=\text { cosntant }...(i)
\end{aligned}$
$\because$ Process equation for adiabatic process is
$\mathrm{PV}^{\mathrm{y}}=\text { constant }...(ii)$
Comparing equation (1) and (2)
$\frac{C_P}{C_V}=y=\frac{3}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> volume cylinder is filled with <math><mn>1</mn></math> mol of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> gas at room temperature <math><mo>(</mo><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> . The molecular diameter of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , and its root mean square speed, are found to be <math><mn>0.3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , respectively. What is the average collision rate (per second) for an <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> molecule?
question: A <math><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> volume cylinder is filled with <math><mn>1</mn></math> mol of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> gas at room temperature <math><mo>(</mo><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> . The molecular diameter of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , and its root mean square speed, are found to be <math><mn>0.3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , respectively. What is the average collision rate (per second) for an <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> molecule?
solution:
Average collision rate =nv- d2π
=6.023×102325×10-3×83π×200×0.3×10-92π≈4×10-12

subject: Physics
q: One kg of a diatomic gas is at a pressure of $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The density of the gas is $4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{-3}$. What is the energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
question: One kg of a diatomic gas is at a pressure of $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The density of the gas is $4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{-3}$. What is the energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
solution:
Thermal energy corresponds to internal energy
Mass $=1 \mathrm{~kg}$
density $=8 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$
$\Rightarrow$ Volume $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1}{8} \mathrm{~m}^3$
Pressure $=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$
$\therefore$ Internal Energy $=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{V}=5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II:

  List I   List II
(A) 3 Translational degrees of freedom (I) Monoatomic gases
(B) 3 Translational, 2 rotational degrees of freedoms (II) Polyatomic gases
(C) 3 Translational, 2 rotational and 1 vibrational degrees of freedom (III) Rigid diatomic gases
(D) 3 Translational, 3 rotational and more than one vibrational degrees of freedom (IV) Nonrigid diatomic gases

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II:

  List I   List II
(A) 3 Translational degrees of freedom (I) Monoatomic gases
(B) 3 Translational, 2 rotational degrees of freedoms (II) Polyatomic gases
(C) 3 Translational, 2 rotational and 1 vibrational degrees of freedom (III) Rigid diatomic gases
(D) 3 Translational, 3 rotational and more than one vibrational degrees of freedom (IV) Nonrigid diatomic gases

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The formula for degree of freedom is given by 

f=3N-k

where N is the number of particles, k is the individual relationship in the system.

For a monoatomic gas particle, N=1, k=0. So, f=3

Hence, monoatomic gas has 3 translational degree of freedom.

For a rigid diatomic gas molecule, there are 5 degrees of freedom, 3 translational and 2 rotational. A diatomic gas can have two extra degrees of freedom due to rotation along two independent axes.

A non rigid diatomic molecule has one extra degree of freedom than a rigid one. This is the vibrational degree of freedom. Hence, it has 6 degrees of freedom.

So, a non rigid diatomic gas has 3 translational, 2 rotational and 1 vibrational degrees of freedom.

A polyatomic gas has more than two atoms per molecule. Hence, the total number of degrees of freedom is 3 translational,  3 rotational and more than one vibrational degree of freedom.

q: Two vessels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are of the same size and are at same temperature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are the pressures of the gases in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
question: Two vessels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are of the same size and are at same temperature. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are the pressures of the gases in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
For both the gases, it follows that

PAVAPBVB=nARTAnBRTB   ...1

Given VA=VB and TA=TB.

Thus, from equation (1), it follows that

PAPB=nAnB=12132=16

subject: Physics
q: Three perfect gases at absolute temperatures $T_1, T_2$ and $T_3$ are mixed. The masses of molecules are $m_1$, $m_2$ and $m_3$ and the number of molecules are $n_1, n_2$ and $n_3$ respectively. Assuming no loss of energy, the final temperature of the mixture is :
question: Three perfect gases at absolute temperatures $T_1, T_2$ and $T_3$ are mixed. The masses of molecules are $m_1$, $m_2$ and $m_3$ and the number of molecules are $n_1, n_2$ and $n_3$ respectively. Assuming no loss of energy, the final temperature of the mixture is :
solution:
Data $\Rightarrow n, k, t_1+n_2 k T_2+n_3 k T_3=\left(n_1+n_2+n_3\right) k T$
$\therefore T=\frac{n_1 T_1+n_2 T_2+n_3 T_3}{n_1+n_2+n_3}$

subject: Physics
q: A polyatomic ideal gas has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> vibrational modes. What is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: A polyatomic ideal gas has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> vibrational modes. What is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
Since each vibrational mode has 2 degrees of freedom hence total vibrational degrees of freedom =48

f=3+3+48=54

γ=1+2f=2827=1.03

q: Three vessels of equal volume contain gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monoatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic) and third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). Arrange these on the basis of their root mean square speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mrow><mi>r</mi><mi>m</mi><mi>s</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> and choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question: Three vessels of equal volume contain gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monoatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic) and third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). Arrange these on the basis of their root mean square speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mrow><mi>r</mi><mi>m</mi><mi>s</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> and choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The RMS speed is given vrms =3RTM.

The temperature and pressure is same for all the gases. So the speed is inversely proportional to the square root of the molar mass of the gases.

vrms(mono)=3RT4×10-3vrms(dia)=3RT71×10-3vrms(poly)=3RT352×10-3

vrms ∝1M

Hence,

vrms(poly)<vrms(dia)<vrms(mono).

(<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)

=12M3kTm

⇒vrms2=819kM

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of vapour densities of two gases at the same temperature is $\frac{4}{25}$, then the ratio of r.m.s. velocities will be:
question: The ratio of vapour densities of two gases at the same temperature is $\frac{4}{25}$, then the ratio of r.m.s. velocities will be:
solution:
(Vapor density) $=\left(\frac{\text { Molar mass }}{2}\right)$, I.e, $v d=\frac{M}{2}$
$\begin{aligned} & v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} \\ & \frac{v_2}{v_1}=\sqrt{\frac{M_1}{M_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{4}}=\frac{5}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: What will be the average value of energy for a monoatomic gas in thermal equilibrium at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: What will be the average value of energy for a monoatomic gas in thermal equilibrium at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
As per Equi-partition law,

each degree of freedom contributes
12 kBT average energy.

In mono atomic gas, D.O.F.=3
⇒ Average energy =3×12 kBT=32 kBT

subject: Physics
q: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> moles of oxygen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> moles of neon at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system will be:
question: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> moles of oxygen and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> moles of neon at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system will be:
solution:
The internal energy of a gas is given by U=nCVT.

A diatomic gas has 3 translational and 2 rotational degrees of freedom. A monoatomic gas has 3 translational degrees of freedom.

Hence, the internal energy of the mixture =n1CV1T+n2CV2T

=2×5R2T+4×3R2T=5RT+6RT

=11RT

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of an open room of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> increases from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> due to the sunshine. The atmospheric pressure in the room remains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub></math> are the number of molecules in the room before and after heating, then <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> will be:
question: The temperature of an open room of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> increases from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> due to the sunshine. The atmospheric pressure in the room remains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub></math> are the number of molecules in the room before and after heating, then <math><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub></math> will be:
solution:
Ti=273+17=290K

Tf=273+27=300K

From ideal gas equation, PV=nRT ⇒n=PVRT

nf-ni=PVR 1Tf-1Ti= -2.5×1025

subject: Physics
q: For an ideal gas the instantaneous change in pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> with volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is given by the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is the given boundary condition, then the maximum temperature one mole of gas can attain is: (Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the gas constant)
question: For an ideal gas the instantaneous change in pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> with volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is given by the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>V</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is the given boundary condition, then the maximum temperature one mole of gas can attain is: (Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the gas constant)
solution:
dPdV=-aP
∫P0PdPP=-∫0vadV⇒lnPP0=-aV
P=P0e-aV
Now, PV=RT(as n=1)
P+VdPdV=RdTdV
[ for maximum T, dTdV=0]
P-aPV=0⇒V=1a
Thus, T=PVR=P0e-1Ra=P0eRa

subject: Physics
q: $\gamma_{\mathrm{A}}$ is the specific heat ratio of monoatomic gas A having 3 translational degrees of freedom. $\gamma_B$ is the specific heat ratio of polyatomic gas B having 3 translational, 3 rotational degrees of freedom and 1 vibrational mode. If $\frac{\gamma_{\mathrm{A}}}{\gamma_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}\right)$, then the value of $n$ is _______.
question: $\gamma_{\mathrm{A}}$ is the specific heat ratio of monoatomic gas A having 3 translational degrees of freedom. $\gamma_B$ is the specific heat ratio of polyatomic gas B having 3 translational, 3 rotational degrees of freedom and 1 vibrational mode. If $\frac{\gamma_{\mathrm{A}}}{\gamma_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}\right)$, then the value of $n$ is _______.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\gamma_A}{\gamma_B}=\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{A}}+2}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{A}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{B}}+2} \\ & =\frac{3+2}{3} \times \frac{(6+2)}{(6+2)+2} \\ & =\frac{5}{3} \times \frac{8}{10}=\frac{40}{30} \\ & \because \frac{40}{30}=1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{40}{30}-1=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a mixture of gas molecule of types <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> having masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of their root mean square speeds at normal temperature and pressure is:
question: Consider a mixture of gas molecule of types <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> having masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub><mo><</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of their root mean square speeds at normal temperature and pressure is:
solution:
Vrms=3RTM
mA<mB<mC
⇒VA>VB>VC
⇒1 VA<1 VB<1 VC

question: Cooking gas containers are kept in a lorry moving with uniform speed. The temperature of the gas molecules inside will

It is observed that, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>v</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi></math> for hydrogen gas and <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi></math> for nitrogen gas. The correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> is:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>v</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></math> are specific heats at constant pressure and constant volume respectively. It is observed that, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>v</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi></math> for hydrogen gas and <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi></math> for nitrogen gas. The correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> is:
solution:
Cp-Cv=RM

For hydrogen Cp-Cv=R2=a

For nitrogen Cp-Cv=R28=b

ab=14

a=14b

question:
The average kinetic energy of a monatomic molecule is 0.414 eV at temperature:

(Use KB=1.38×10-23 J mol-1 K-1)
solution:

The formula for the average kinetic energy is given by

Kavg=12f KBT   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

0.414 eV×1.6×10-19 J1 eV=32×1.38×10-23 J mol-1 K-1×T⇒T=0.414 eV×1.6×10-19 J1 eV×23×1.38×10-23 J mol-1 K-1=3200 K

subject: Physics
q:
Following statements are given
(1) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases when the temperature is reduced.
(2) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule increases with increase in pressure at constant temperature.
(3) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases with increases in volume.
(4) Pressure of a gas increases with increase in temperature at constant volume.
(5) The volume of gas decreases with increase in temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Following statements are given
(1) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases when the temperature is reduced.
(2) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule increases with increase in pressure at constant temperature.
(3) The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule decreases with increases in volume.
(4) Pressure of a gas increases with increase in temperature at constant volume.
(5) The volume of gas decreases with increase in temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Average kinetic energy of a gas molecule is given by KEavg =32KT.

Pressure of a gas in terms of rms speed is given by P=13ρvrms2.

As KEavg∝T, so 1 is correct, 2 is incorrect, statement 3 can not be said.

Note : Statement (4) is correct only if we consider it at constant volume and not constant pressure. Ideally, this question must be bonus but most appropriate answer is option (A)

solution:
Recall the formula of root mean square speed, vRMS=3RTM, 

⇒vRMSO2 vRMSH2=MH2 MO2=232=14

⇒vRMSH2=4 vRMSO2=4×160=640 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Consider two ideal diatomic gases <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> at some temperature <math><mi>T</mi></math> . Molecules of the gas <math><mi>A</mi></math> are rigid, and have a mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> . Molecules of the gas <math><mi>B</mi></math> have an additional vibrational mode and have a mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> . The ratio of the specific heats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><msubsup><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi><mo> </mo></msubsup></mfenced><mi>A</mi></msub><mtext>and </mtext><msub><mfenced><mrow><mo> </mo><msubsup><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi><mrow/></msubsup></mrow></mfenced><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of gas <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively is:
question: Consider two ideal diatomic gases <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> at some temperature <math><mi>T</mi></math> . Molecules of the gas <math><mi>A</mi></math> are rigid, and have a mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> . Molecules of the gas <math><mi>B</mi></math> have an additional vibrational mode and have a mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> . The ratio of the specific heats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><msubsup><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi><mo> </mo></msubsup></mfenced><mi>A</mi></msub><mtext>and </mtext><msub><mfenced><mrow><mo> </mo><msubsup><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi><mrow/></msubsup></mrow></mfenced><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of gas <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively is:
solution:
Molar heat capacity of A at constant volume =5R2
Molar heat capacity of B at constant volume =7R2
Dividing both CvACvB=57

subject: Physics
q: The number density of molecules of a gas depends on their distance r from the origin as, <math><mi>n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then the numer of molecules is proportional to:
question: The number density of molecules of a gas depends on their distance r from the origin as, <math><mi>n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then the numer of molecules is proportional to:
solution:
Density of molecules of gas as a of r is, n(r)=n0e-αr4
Total no. of molecules =∫0∞n(r)dv
∫0∞n0 e-αr44πr2 dr
To integrate this, substitute u=α3/4r3  this will give us dr=13α3/4r2du
Now, the integration will become, 4πn03α3/4 ∫0∞ e-u4/3du
This shows that the numer of molecules is proportional to n0α-3/4

subject: Physics
q: Calculate the value of the mean free path <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>λ</mi></mfenced></math> for oxygen molecules at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. Assume the molecular diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and the gas is ideal. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Calculate the value of the mean free path <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>λ</mi></mfenced></math> for oxygen molecules at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. Assume the molecular diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and the gas is ideal. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
λ=RT2πd2 NAP

λ=8.314×3002×3.14×0.3×10-92×6.023×1023×1.01×105

λ=102 nm

subject: Physics
q: If three moles of monoatomic gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is mixed with two moles of a diatomic gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, the value of adiabatic exponent <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> for the mixture is:
question: If three moles of monoatomic gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is mixed with two moles of a diatomic gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, the value of adiabatic exponent <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> for the mixture is:
solution:
Given:

f1=3,f2=5n1=3,n2=2

Now, for mixture

fmixture =n1f1+n2f2n1+n2=9+105=195

Therefore, γmixture =1+2fmixture=1+2×519=2919=1.52

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of diatomic gas in a cylinder is at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Heat is supplied to the cylinder such that the temperature remains constant but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> moles of the diatomic gas get converted into monoatomic gas. The change in the total kinetic energy of the gas is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of diatomic gas in a cylinder is at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Heat is supplied to the cylinder such that the temperature remains constant but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> moles of the diatomic gas get converted into monoatomic gas. The change in the total kinetic energy of the gas is
solution:
Initial kinetic energy of the system,

Ui=N52RT

Final kinetic energy of the system,

Uf=2n32 RT+N-n52RT

⇒Uf=12nRT+52NRT

ΔU=Q=12nRT.
